rows: Neuron
Basic functional unit of the nervous system that transmits electrical and chemical signals.
Dendrites receive input, axon transmits output to synapse.
Action potential
Rapid depolarization and repolarization of the neuronal membrane that propagates a signal.
Triggered when threshold is reached; Na+ influx, then K+ efflux.
Synapse
Junction between two neurons where neurotransmitters are released to communicate.
Presynaptic neuron releases vesicles containing neurotransmitters into the synaptic cleft.
Dopamine
Neurotransmitter involved in reward, motivation, and motor control.
Parkinson's disease involves dopamine loss; addiction involves dopamine dysregulation.
Serotonin
Neurotransmitter that regulates mood, appetite, and sleep.
Low serotonin levels are linked to depression; SSRIs increase synaptic serotonin.
Central nervous system (CNS)
Part of the nervous system consisting of the brain and spinal cord.
Integrates and processes information; protected by meninges and blood-brain barrier.
Peripheral nervous system (PNS)
Nerves and ganglia outside the brain and spinal cord.
Divided into somatic (voluntary) and autonomic (involuntary) systems.
Sympathetic nervous system
Division of autonomic PNS that mobilizes the body for 'fight or flight'.
Increases heart rate, dilates pupils, inhibits digestion.
Parasympathetic nervous system
Division of autonomic PNS that promotes 'rest and digest'.
Slows heart rate, stimulates digestion, constricts pupils.
Frontal lobe
Brain region responsible for executive functions, planning, and impulse control.
Phineas Gage's damage to frontal lobe altered personality.
Limbic system
Set of brain structures involved in emotion, memory, and motivation.
Includes amygdala (fear/aggression) and hippocampus (memory formation).
Hypothalamus
Brain structure that maintains homeostasis and regulates endocrine function via pituitary gland.
Controls body temperature, hunger, thirst, and circadian rhythms.
Broca's area
Region in left frontal lobe responsible for speech production.
Damage causes expressive aphasia (know what to say but cannot produce fluent speech).
Wernicke's area
Region in left temporal lobe responsible for language comprehension.
Damage causes receptive aphasia (fluent but nonsensical speech, poor comprehension).
Heritability
Proportion of phenotypic variance in a population due to genetic differences.
Heritability of IQ is estimated around 0.5; does not apply to individuals.

questions.text: The basic functional unit of the nervous system that transmits electrical and chemical signals is the ________.
The rapid depolarization and repolarization of the neuronal membrane that propagates a signal is called the ________.
The junction between two neurons where neurotransmitters are released to communicate is the ________.
The neurotransmitter involved in reward, motivation, and motor control, which is lost in Parkinson's disease, is ________.
The neurotransmitter that regulates mood, appetite, and sleep and is targeted by SSRIs for depression is ________.
The part of the nervous system consisting of the brain and spinal cord is the ________.
Nerves and ganglia outside the brain and spinal cord form the ________.
The division of the autonomic nervous system that mobilizes the body for 'fight or flight' is the ________ nervous system.
The division of the autonomic nervous system that promotes 'rest and digest' is the ________ nervous system.
The brain lobe responsible for executive functions, planning, and impulse control, and damaged in Phineas Gage, is the ________ lobe.
The set of brain structures involved in emotion, memory, and motivation, including the amygdala and hippocampus, is the ________ system.
The brain structure that maintains homeostasis and regulates the endocrine system via the pituitary gland is the ________.
The region in the left frontal lobe responsible for speech production, damaged in expressive aphasia, is ________ area.
The region in the left temporal lobe responsible for language comprehension, damaged in receptive aphasia, is ________ area.
The proportion of phenotypic variance in a population due to genetic differences is called ________.
